left.claim: Digital technology use has a negative but very small association with adolescent well-being (at most 0.4% of variance); effects too small to warrant policy change.
left.population: 3 large-scale datasets, total n=355,358 adolescents (specific dataset names/age ranges not returned by fetched source)
left.exposure: Self-reported digital technology use (item wording not stated in fetched abstract)
left.outcome: Adolescent psychological well-being (scale not stated in fetched abstract)
left.design: Specification curve analysis (SCA) across all three datasets to test the association under many reasonable analytic specifications

right.claim: US adolescents are in a mental health crisis since ~2012, and digital media use may be a contributing mechanism; the same underlying data (e.g. Millennium Cohort Study) shows a larger effect than Orben & Przybylski report once analyzed differently.
right.population: Synthesis of large, mostly nationally representative US adolescent/young-adult screening studies since 2010; no single defined sample (narrative review)
right.exposure: Leisure-time digital media use: social media, online gaming, texting, other online activity, streaming video (excludes school/work use)
right.outcome: Depression, anxiety, loneliness, self-harm, suicidal ideation, suicide attempts, suicide rates, psychological well-being
right.time_horizon: ~2000-2018 trend window, focused on the post-2010/2012 shift
right.design: Narrative review of trend data across existing studies; separately cites a reanalysis (Kelly et al.) of the same Millennium Cohort Study data using r rather than r-squared

evidence.span: negative but small, explaining at most 0.4% of the variation in well-being
r=0.20 is twice that of r=0.10, but, after squaring, 4% is four times as large as 1%, leading Funder and Ozer to describe the r-squared approach as misleading

Kelly et al. found that twice as many heavy users of social media (versus nonusers) had clinical levels of depressive symptoms in the Millennium Cohort Study, the same data set that Orben and Przybylski studied

notes: Paper B explicitly reanalyzes/critiques Paper A's statistical framing (r vs r-squared) applied to overlapping underlying data (Millennium Cohort Study). Classified apparent_contradiction rather than genuine_unresolved_conflict because the disagreement is substantially attributable to analytic-choice/design, not to genuinely incompatible findings about the same fixed scope. Not independently verified beyond what the two fetched sources state; dataset names/exact exposure wording for paper_a were not returned by the fetch tool and are marked accordingly.

left.claim: Adopting expansive (high-power) vs contractive (low-power) poses causes physiological (increased testosterone, decreased cortisol) and behavioral (increased risk tolerance) changes.
left.population: N=42; demographic breakdown not stated in fetched abstract
left.exposure: Two 1-minute expansive poses vs two 1-minute contractive poses
left.outcome: Salivary testosterone and cortisol; behavioral risk tolerance (gambling-style task)
left.time_horizon: Single session; poses held 1 minute each; post-pose tracking duration not stated
left.design: Randomized experiment; pre/post saliva hormone assay; behavioral risk-tolerance task

right.claim: Self-reported feelings of power replicate, but hormonal (testosterone, cortisol) and behavioral (risk tolerance) effects of power posing do not replicate in a larger, blinded, preregistered sample.
right.population: N=200 (98 women, 102 men) [secondary source, primary abstract unavailable]
right.exposure: High-power vs low-power poses held 2 minutes each; experimenters blinded to condition [secondary source]
right.outcome: Salivary testosterone and cortisol; risk tolerance via economic decision tasks (gain and loss domains); self-reported feelings of power [secondary source]
right.design: Preregistered, blinded, higher-powered conceptual replication of Carney et al.'s design

evidence.span: posing in displays of power caused advantaged and adaptive psychological, physiological, and behavioral changes
no significant effects on testosterone (mean difference = -4.077, p = 0.162), cortisol (mean difference = -0.028, p = 0.272), or risk tolerance in gain or loss domains

notes: Same construct, same outcome measures, same single-session design as the original -- this is a close/direct replication attempt, not a differently scoped study, so classified genuine_unresolved_conflict rather than apparent_contradiction. Sample size (5x larger) and blinding are the leading candidate explanations for the discrepancy, which is why design and population are both flagged as root dimensions; treat as a calibration test case, not a clean scope difference. Right-paper primary abstract (PubMed) returned 'no abstract available'; N, exact effect sizes, and blinding details are sourced from a secondary write-up, not verified against the primary text -- recommend a human reviewer confirm against the original PDF before this record leaves pending-human-review.

left.claim: Adults should continue current unprocessed red meat and processed meat consumption (weak recommendation, low-certainty evidence).
left.population: Adults (general population); evidence base = 5 linked systematic reviews; guideline panel of 14 members (3 community representatives) from 7 countries
left.exposure: Current levels of unprocessed red meat and processed meat consumption vs reduced consumption
left.outcome: Health outcomes including cardiovascular disease and cancer, rated via GRADE certainty-of-evidence framework
left.time_horizon: not stated as a fixed window; synthesizes available longitudinal/cohort evidence
left.design: 5 de novo systematic reviews plus GRADE methodology to grade certainty of evidence and translate it into a graded practice recommendation

right.claim: Processed meat is classified Group 1 (carcinogenic to humans); red meat is classified Group 2A (probably carcinogenic to humans); risk increases with consumption quantity.
right.population: 800+ epidemiological/cancer studies reviewed (700+ on red meat, 400+ on processed meat); evaluated by 22 experts from 10 countries
right.exposure: Consumption of processed meat and red meat (quantity-dependent)
right.outcome: Cancer hazard classification -- primarily colorectal cancer for both; also stomach (processed) and pancreatic/prostate (red meat)
right.time_horizon: not stated (aggregated across studies with varying follow-up)
right.design: IARC standard hazard-classification process: evaluates strength of evidence that an agent is a cause of cancer; explicitly not a quantified risk-at-exposure-level assessment

evidence.span: The panel suggests that adults continue current unprocessed red meat consumption (weak recommendation, low-certainty evidence)
the strength of the scientific evidence about an agent being a cause of cancer, rather than assessing the level of risk

notes: The two sources answer structurally different questions on overlapping evidence: IARC performs categorical hazard identification, NutriRECS performs a GRADE-weighted actionability judgment folding in effect magnitude and certainty. Neither fetched primary source states its underlying risk numbers are quantitatively close to the other's -- that specific claim (raw numbers not being that different) originates from outside commentary (WCRF) referenced by the user, not independently fetched or verified here, so it is not asserted as fact in this record.
